data.title: Teck Resources, Kodiak Copper to spin off copper projects into new U.S.-focused firm
data.description:
Teck Resources (TECK) signed a non-binding letter of intent with Kodiak Copper (KDKCF) to establish a new U.S.-focused copper exploration company, MiningWeekly.com reported Thursday.

As part of the transaction, Kodiak (KDKCF) said it will transfer its wholly owned Mohave project, and Teck (TECK) plans to transfer its fully owned Copper Hill project, both located in Arizona, to a subsidiary of unlisted Kay Copper; the new private company will apply to list on the TSX Venture Exchange.

Kodiak (KDKCF) and Teck (TECK) will each receive 20M shares of the new company at a nominal value of $0.25/share (C$0.34) for their respective projects; ownership distribution likely will result in Kodiak and Teck each holding 28% and existing Kay Copper shareholders owning 9%, with the remaining shares distributed among new financing participants.

The Mohave project includes a 17 sq km area that presents possible porphyry copper-molybdenum-silver mineralization similar to nearby mining sites, although it has not seen drilling since 2011, and Copper Hill, a 35 sq km site, lies within a prolific mining district with underexplored porphyry targets identified by recent exploration.

data.title: Virgin Galactic rises after six straight sessions of losses
data.description:
Virgin Galactic (NYSE: SPCE) traded higher on Thursday afternoon, rising 2.36% to $2.39 and snapping a six-session losing streak.

The stock has dropped more than 20.21% over the past six trading days, underperforming the S&P 500 Index, which gained 0.38% over the same period. Year to date in 2026, shares are down over 25.70%, compared with a 4.25% advance in the broader index.

In the prior month, the company priced spaceflight tickets at $750,000, about $100,000 higher than earlier offerings, as it focuses on developing a more reusable spacecraft after halting commercial flights in June 2024. CEO Michael Colglazier said the first SpaceShip is nearing completion, with ground testing set to begin in April, while a second is expected to enter service in late 2026 or early 2027.

The update follows quarterly results that missed expectations, with the company reporting a loss of $0.98 per share as revenue fell 27.9% year over year to about $312,000.

According to Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings, SPCE carries a “Hold” rating with a score of 2.73 out of 5, reflecting a D- grade for profitability, alongside an A- for growth and an A for valuation.

Wall Street analysts also remain cautious on the stock, assigning it a “Hold” rating.

 However, analyst Bernard Zambonin maintains a more constructive view, calling SPCE a high-risk, high-reward play with potential upside if key execution milestones, including a planned commercial flight in Q4 2026, are achieved.

Similarly, Seeking Alpha analysts appeared rather bullish about the stock, issuing a Buy call for SPCE.

data.title: Bleeding disorder firm Hemab upsizes proposed IPO to 15M shares
data.description:
• Hemab Therapeutics Holdings (COAG), a drug developer targeting blood coagulation disorders, upsized its initial public offering, planning to issue 15M shares of its common stock at $16–$18 per share under the ticker symbol “COAG.” On Nasdaq.
  • The clinical-stage biotech whose backers include the Novo Nordisk Foundation, the controlling shareholder of weight loss drug developer Novo Nordisk (NVO), previously sought to sell approximately 11.8M shares.
  • According to a regulatory filing on Thursday, the Cambridge, Massachusetts-based company has expanded the offering to 15M shares, planning to raise $270M at a valuation of roughly $764M at the top of the price range.
  • Hemab (COAG) intends to use the IPO proceeds primarily to advance its lead candidate, sutacimig, which is currently undergoing early/mid-stage development for two genetically driven bleeding disorders called Glanzmann thrombasthenia and Factor VII deficiency.

data.title: Adamas Trust downgraded at B. Riley Securities despite blowout earnings
data.description:
Adamas Trust (ADAM) was downgraded at B. Riley Securities even as the mortgage REIT's Q1 earnings beat the consensus by a wide margin.

"Investment activity continues to be strong at ADAM, with single-family credit growing 5.6% Q/Q while agency grew 1.4%, which is a divergence from 2025 investing, which was predominantly through the agency channel," said analyst Timothy D'Agostino.

"Given the overall growth in 2025 and 1Q26, including a new investment focus, the closing of Constructive, and an increase to the quarterly dividend in 3Q25, the stock is up 57.5% over the TTM (RUT 39.5%)," said the analyst.

"Given the price action, we move to the sideline and await a better entry point. We tick up our PT (price target) to $9.50 to reflect the increase in BV (book value)," said the research note.

"We are long-term supporters of the platform and believe management has done an excellent job transitioning the investment portfolio, which has led to EAD expansion and a quarterly dividend increase in 3Q25," D'Agostino noted.

B. Riley cut its recommendation on ADAM to Neutral from Buy, but raised the price target to $9.50 from $9.00.

The rating aligns with the average Seeking Alpha authors rating of Hold. Meanwhile, the sell-side analysts grade the stock as Buy.

data.title: Activist Jana renews push for Markel to divest ventures, buy back shares - report
data.description:
Activist Jana Partners is renewing a push for Markel Group (MKL) to divest its venture arm, and it also wants the insurer to repurchase $2 billion of its stock. 

“The current structure produces sub-peer shareholder returns, creates no unique value and warrants a discounted multiple,” Jana Managing Partner Scott Ostfeld and Managing Director Jimmy Ganas said in a letter to Markel’s board Thursday reviewed by Bloomberg News. “The need for change is clear.” 

The renewed push comes after Jana said in December 2024 that Markel (MKL) should explore a separation or sale of its private investments business., Markel Ventures. 

Jana and Markel didn't immediately respond to Bloomberg's request for comment.

Markel (MKL) reported Q1 results on Tuesday.

Shares of Markel rose 1.1% and have dropped 18% this year.

data.title: Labcorp forecasts 2026 adjusted EPS of $17.70 to $18.35 while raising revenue growth outlook to 5% to 6.1%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Labcorp (LH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are off to a strong start in 2026 with continued momentum in both our Diagnostics and Central Laboratory businesses" (President, CEO & Chairman Adam Schechter), adding that "revenue reached $3.5 billion, increasing 6%" and that "adjusted earnings per share grew 11%."
  • "We recently announced a nationwide strategic collaboration with Children's Hospital of Philadelphia to expand access to cutting-edge diagnostics for pediatric patients" (President, CEO & Chairman Schechter), and said Labcorp "completed our acquisition of select assets of Crouse Health's Laboratory Alliance of Central New York" and "remain on track to close our acquisition of select outreach laboratory services across Indiana and Northwest Ohio from Parkview Health in the very near future."
  • "We continue to deliver double-digit growth" in Consumer Health (President, CEO & Chairman Schechter), highlighting that "MyLabcorp, our secure mobile app" is "launching in May" and that "MyLabcorp's AI assistant will also help simplify appointment scheduling and payments."
  • "In the first quarter, enterprise revenue grew 5.8% and enterprise adjusted operating margin expanded more than 30 basis points to 14.4%" (CFO & Executive VP Julia Wang), adding "Adjusted EPS was $4.25" and "we generated $71 million in free cash flow."

Outlook

  • "We are raising the midpoint of the enterprise revenue range by approximately $30 million and the midpoint of the EPS range by $0.13" (CFO & Executive VP Wang).
  • "We expect enterprise revenue to grow 5% to 6.1%" (CFO & Executive VP Wang), while "we expect Diagnostics segment revenue to grow 5.1% to 5.9%" and "we expect BLS segment revenue to grow 3.8% to 5.4%."
  • "Our adjusted EPS guidance range is $17.70 to $18.35" (CFO & Executive VP Wang), and "our free cash flow guidance range remains $1.24 billion to $1.36 billion, weighted towards the second half of the year."

Financial Results

  • "Enterprise revenue was $3.5 billion" with "3.1% organic growth" and "adjusted operating income was $508 million" (CFO & Executive VP Wang).
  • "Diagnostic Laboratories delivered another strong quarter with 5% revenue growth to $2.8 billion" (CFO & Executive VP Wang), stating "Total volume growth was 2.5%" and "Price/mix increased 2.6%."
  • "Biopharma Laboratory Services revenue grew to $781 million, up 8.2%" and "our BLS segment ended the quarter with a backlog of $8.6 billion" (CFO & Executive VP Wang), adding "we expect approximately $2.7 billion to convert into revenue over the next 12 months."

Q&A

  • Lisa Gill, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: Asked about weather impacts and ACA exchange changes; President, CEO & Chairman Schechter said, "we estimate it was about a $15 million impact," while CFO & Executive VP Wang said the ACA effect in Q1 was "really immaterial" but added, "we continue to believe that the 30 basis point volume impact is a good estimate to work with."
  • Jack Meehan, Nephron Research: Asked about CMS "CRUSH" exposure; President, CEO & Chairman Schechter said, "we're supportive of any initiative that can create a level playing field" and added ACLA urged CMS "to avoid unintended consequences" including "impeding Medicare patient access."
  • Michael Cherny, Leerink Partners: Asked about Diagnostics volume vs. mix and share; President, CEO & Chairman Schechter tied mix to specialty growth and said, "I feel confident in the guidance that we've provided," while CFO & Executive VP Wang said, "we continue to expect the majority of that revenue growth to be coming from organically."
  • Patrick Donnelly, Citi: Asked about BLS bookings and confidence in sequential book-to-bill improvement; President, CEO & Chairman Schechter said the Q1 "0.94" book-to-bill was "mostly driven by timing" and added, "I expect to have a book-to-bill that will remain above 1" for the year.
  • Tycho Peterson, Jefferies: Asked about esoteric testing reimbursement and Alzheimer’s testing channel strategy; President, CEO & Chairman Schechter said oncology reimbursement "aren't necessarily quite where we'd like them to be" and added on Alzheimer’s, "we will break it out because it is growing so quickly" (while noting, "We don't yet break out the individual segments").
  • Elizabeth Anderson, Evercore ISI: Asked about PAMA survey participation and RESULTS Act; President, CEO & Chairman Schechter said, "we're really waiting for a CBO score" and emphasized PAMA impact depends on reporting: "The more that report, the lower the impact will be for Labcorp."
  • David Westenberg, Piper Sandler: Asked about consumer investments and ROI; President, CEO & Chairman Schechter said, "we already do some advertising" and added, "there are some parts of it... we're not necessarily going to compete at this time" due to pricing uncertainty.
  • Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities: Asked about LaunchPad and margin pacing; President, CEO & Chairman Schechter said, "we're on track," and CFO & Executive VP Wang said, "we continue to expect another year of margin improvement in Diagnostics" and that "BLS margin" is expected "to improve more than that for Diagnostics."
  • Anna Kruszenski, Barclays: Asked about fuel inflation and further weather assumptions; CFO & Executive VP Wang said the fuel AOI impact estimate is "approximately $5 million to $10 million this year" and added, "we generally do not bake in explicit assumption for weather."
  • Erin Wright, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the deal pipeline and comparisons to last year; President, CEO & Chairman Schechter said, "Our pipeline remains very strong" and "I wouldn't say it's accelerated versus this time last year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, emphasizing policy and utilization uncertainty ("potential changes" to ACA exchanges; "CRUSH" exposure; PAMA participation) and near-term levers (bookings, margins, fuel, weather).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and confident in execution and guidance, with repeated language such as "I feel good about" and "I feel confident" (President, CEO & Chairman Schechter) and "we are really pleased" and "we continue to expect" (CFO & Executive VP Wang).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management’s confidence language was more explicitly tied to raised guidance ("raising the midpoint") versus Q4’s framing of an outlook that "reflects the delay of PAMA through December 31, 2026" (President, CEO & Chairman Schechter in Q4 2025).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted upward: Q1 raised 2026 midpoints ("approximately $30 million" for revenue and "$0.13" for EPS), while Q4 initially guided to "revenue to grow 4.7% to 6%" and adjusted EPS "$17.55 to $18.25" (CFO & Executive VP Wang in Q4 2025).
  • Analysts’ policy focus evolved from PAMA delay mechanics in Q4 to practicalities of upcoming reporting and related initiatives in Q1 (PAMA survey timing; "CRUSH" fraud-and-abuse initiative).
  • Strategic emphasis stayed consistent (hospital partnerships, specialty diagnostics, AI/automation), with Q1 adding more explicit commercialization and consumer-interface milestones, including "MyLabcorp" launching "in May" (President, CEO & Chairman Schechter).

Risks and Concerns

  • "We estimate [weather] was about a $15 million impact" (President, CEO & Chairman Schechter), with recapture limits acknowledged for physician-office testing: "There, we don't necessarily know who has appointments" (President, CEO & Chairman Schechter).
  • "We continue to believe that the 30 basis point volume impact is a good estimate" from ACA dynamics (CFO & Executive VP Wang), while noting Q1 impact was "immaterial" and dependent on premiums paid and utilization.
  • On reimbursement and policy, management flagged uncertainty around PAMA outcomes and participation: "The impact to Labcorp will be highly dependent upon the number of other laboratories, including hospital laboratories that report their data" (President, CEO & Chairman Schechter).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a "strong start" supported by Diagnostics and Central Labs momentum, margin expansion, and continued capital deployment, and it raised 2026 guidance midpoints while reiterating focus on hospital partnerships, specialty testing growth, and technology-driven efficiencies. In Q&A, analysts concentrated on policy-driven volume and pricing variables (ACA exchanges, CRUSH, PAMA/RESULTS Act) and near-term operational sensitivities (weather, fuel, bookings), while management repeatedly characterized guidance and execution as on track and monitored risks as "manageable" within the updated 2026 ranges.
